narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 44% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 2—much lower than the typical student's 5.7 and 5.5—along with your LSAT score of 25.5, well below the average of 36.7. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 is actually a bit stronger than the average of 3.21, your middle family income aligns closely with peers, and as a full-time male Hispanic student, you fit within common demographic patterns, though Hispanic applicants make up just 3.9% of the pool. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile ranking, which pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure more than any other factor. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which also significantly nudged the outlook toward failure compared to higher-scoring applicants. Your Year 1 decile ranking came next in influence, similarly contributing to the concern about passing the bar. Factors like family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, and gender followed in descending order of impact, each tilting slightly toward the risk of failure in your case. The one positive note was your choice to attend full-time, which helped pull the prediction a bit toward success, unlike part-time students who face steeper odds. Overall, your profile stands out for lower early academic deciles and LSAT performance relative to the dataset's stronger averages, putting you at higher predicted risk than most applicants who get approved. To improve your chances next time, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward or above 36.7, as it's a highly changeable factor with big influence. Strengthening your later academic deciles through better coursework or additional prep could also shift future predictions favorably. Your solid UGPA is already a strength, so building on that while addressing these key areas will make a real difference in showing the model—and admissions—you're bar-exam ready. Keep pushing; many students turn things around with targeted effort.
